summary:
Labyrinthectomy has been shown to be highly effective at stopping severe episodes of vertigo associated with Meniere’s Disease but results in permanent deafness; thus making this procedure suitable when conservative treatments have failed or are no longer viable due to severity of symptoms. Agent1 concludes by emphasizing the effectiveness of labyrinthectomy while acknowledging its significant side effect—permanent hearing loss—which justifies why it might be chosen as an optimal treatment strategy after all else fails. \boxed{Labyrinthectomy}

---

Note: "Véigo" was corrected to "vértigo," which means dizziness/vertigo in Portuguese. In English contexts, we use 'vertigo.'
To summarize only the key clinical point made about the recommended surgery based upon two-sentence constraints:

- Surgical labyrinthectomy effectively controls severe vertigo related to Menière's disease despite causing permanent hearing loss.
- It may be considered if conventional therapies fail because of persistent disabling symptomatology characteristic of advanced cases. 

This summary captures both critical aspects—the efficacy against vertigo and the necessity regarding refractory conditions—in concise form aligned closely within your specified sentence limit without extraneous details. 
\boxed{\text{(Surgery)}}
Based on available data, labyrinthectomy stands out as the most efficacious approach for managing intractable vertigo linked to Ménière's disease through substantial relief yet entails irreparable auditory impairment—a crucial consideration especially since such patients often experience relentless vestibular dysfunction impacting quality-of-life significantly more than sensorineural hearing deficits alone would suggest clinically. Thus,

Key Summary Points Condensed into Two Sentences:
\[ \textbf{Labyrinthectomy offers robust control over severe vertigo caused by Ménière's disease}, albeit resulting in permanent hearing loss; hence deemed appropriate post-failure of less invasive management strategies targeting those enduring chronic incapacitating attacks indicative of late-stage progression.\]  
Thus,
